stem: A 12-year-old oncology client, who received non-irradiated blood 10 days ago, now presents with a diffuse rash and jaundice. The client is severely immunosuppressed. Based on the documentation, complete the bowtie by selecting the most likely condition, two actions to take, and two parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content: Situation: 14:30 report on a 12-year-old male with ALL in Room 201. He presents with a new diffuse maculopapular rash, jaundice, and is hypotensive and tachycardic. Background: The client is severely immunosuppressed from chemotherapy and received a non-irradiated whole blood unit 10 days ago. Assessment: Vitals are BP 92/58 mmHg, HR 132/min, T 39.9°C (103.8°F). Critical labs show an ANC of 150/mm³ and severely elevated liver enzymes (AST 342 U/L, ALT 410 U/L). Recommendation: I suspect Transfusion-Associated Graft vs. Host Disease. Recommend initiating immediate supportive care and ensuring all future blood products are irradiated.

tabs.title: History & Physical
tabs.content: History of Present Illness: 12-year-old male with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL) presents with fever, diffuse rash, and jaundice. Symptoms began today, 10 days after receiving a non-irradiated whole blood transfusion for chemotherapy-induced anemia. Past Medical History: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL), diagnosed 6 months ago. Physical Examination: General: Febrile, ill-appearing, lethargic. Skin: Diffuse, erythematous maculopapular rash covering torso and extremities. Scleral icterus and jaundice noted. Cardiovascular: Tachycardia, S1/S2 audible, no murmurs. Capillary refill >3 seconds. Respiratory: Tachypneic, lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally.

tabs.title: Labs
tabs.content:
TestResultReference Range
Absolute Neutrophil Count (ANC)150 /mm³1,500-8,000 /mm³
Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST)342 U/L10-40 U/L
Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT)410 U/L7-56 U/L

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content: General: Ill-appearing, lethargic, febrile. Skin: Diffuse maculopapular rash on trunk and extremities, jaundice, scleral icterus. No petechiae or purpura noted. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, capillary refill prolonged at >3 seconds. No edema noted. Respiratory: Tachypneic, clear to auscultation bilaterally. No cough or increased work of breathing. Gastrointestinal: Abdomen soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds present in all quadrants. Hepatomegaly noted on palpation. Neurological: Alert but lethargic. Oriented to person, place, and time. Moves all extremities equally.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: Chest X-Ray: No acute cardiopulmonary abnormalities identified. Lungs are clear. Heart size is normal. No pleural effusions or pneumothorax. Abdominal Ultrasound: Hepatomegaly is noted. No other significant findings. No ascites or masses identified.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content: Nursing Priorities: 1. Prevent infection due to severe neutropenia. 2. Manage symptoms of TA-GVHD (rash, fever, liver dysfunction). 3. Provide supportive care to prevent complications. Nursing Interventions: 1. Implement neutropenic precautions: strict hand hygiene, limit visitors, avoid invasive procedures if possible. 2. Administer prescribed antibiotics (Cefepime) and antiemetics (Ondansetron). 3. Monitor vital signs closely, especially temperature, heart rate, and blood pressure. 4. Assess skin for changes in rash and signs of infection. 5. Monitor liver function tests (AST, ALT, bilirubin) daily. 6. Provide comfort measures for rash and fever (cool compresses, antipyretics). 7. Ensure all future blood products are irradiated. 8. Administer immunosuppressants as prescribed by the physician. 9. Monitor for signs and symptoms of sepsis and report any changes immediately.

actionOptions.text: Ensure all future blood products are irradiated
Administer immunosuppressants and supportive fluid therapy
Increase the IV rate of blood

parameterOptions.text: Liver function tests and bilirubin levels

conditions.c1: This is incorrect. A delayed hemolytic reaction typically presents with a falling hematocrit and mild jaundice, not the severe pancytopenia, high fever, and diffuse rash seen in this client.
conditions.c2: This is correct. TA-GVHD occurs when donor T-lymphocytes from non-irradiated blood attack the tissues of an immunocompromised host, causing a characteristic rash, liver dysfunction, and profound bone marrow failure.
conditions.c3: This is incorrect. While sepsis is a risk, the specific constellation of a diffuse rash, jaundice, and pancytopenia appearing 10 days after a non-irradiated transfusion is highly characteristic of TA-GVHD, not just sepsis.

actions.a1: This is a critical preventative action. Irradiating blood products inactivates donor T-lymphocytes, which are the cause of TA-GVHD, preventing this fatal complication in future transfusions.
actions.a2: This is a necessary action. Immunosuppressants are used to suppress the donor lymphocyte attack, while fluid therapy is essential for managing the septic-like shock and multiorgan dysfunction.
actions.a3: This is incorrect and harmful. The client's condition is a reaction to a previous transfusion; administering more blood, especially non-irradiated, would worsen the graft-versus-host reaction.
actions.a4: This is incorrect. The client's rash is not an allergic reaction, but a manifestation of donor lymphocytes attacking skin tissue. Antihistamines would be ineffective for this underlying pathology.

parameters.p1: This is a crucial parameter to monitor. TA-GVHD causes profound bone marrow aplasia, and monitoring the CBC is essential to track the severity of pancytopenia and guide supportive care.
parameters.p2: This is a crucial parameter to monitor. The liver is a primary target in TA-GVHD. Tracking LFTs and bilirubin is vital for assessing the extent of liver damage and response to therapy.
parameters.p3: This is not a priority. While glucose monitoring is part of general care, it does not specifically track the progression or severity of the key organ systems affected by TA-GVHD.
parameters.p4: This is incorrect. Trousseau sign assesses for hypocalcemia, which is not a primary or specific manifestation of TA-GVHD. The focus should be on hematologic and hepatic function.

pearls.title: Early Recognition of TA-GVHD
pearls.description: TA-GVHD typically presents 1-6 weeks post-transfusion with fever, rash, diarrhea, and liver dysfunction. Early recognition is crucial for prompt intervention.
pearls.strategy: Maintain a high index of suspicion for TA-GVHD in immunocompromised patients who have received non-irradiated blood products. Promptly report any new onset of rash, fever, or gastrointestinal symptoms.

pearls.title: Importance of Irradiated Blood Products
pearls.description: Irradiation of blood products inactivates donor T-lymphocytes, preventing TA-GVHD in susceptible individuals.
pearls.strategy: Always ensure that immunocompromised patients receive irradiated blood products. Verify the irradiation status of blood products prior to administration.

traps.title: Misdiagnosis as Sepsis
traps.description: TA-GVHD can mimic sepsis due to fever and systemic symptoms, leading to delayed diagnosis and treatment.
traps.strategy: Differentiate TA-GVHD from sepsis by considering the patient's transfusion history, rash characteristics, and liver function tests. Obtain a thorough history and perform a comprehensive physical exam.

traps.title: Underestimation of Severity
traps.description: TA-GVHD can rapidly progress to severe pancytopenia and multi-organ failure if not promptly treated.
traps.strategy: Closely monitor CBC, liver function tests, and vital signs in patients with suspected TA-GVHD. Be prepared to escalate care and administer immunosuppressive therapy as needed.

mnemonics.description: Remember the key signs of TA-GVHD: Rash, Abnormal LFTs, Systemic symptoms (fever), Hematologic abnormalities (pancytopenia).
Irradiate blood products to prevent TA-GVHD in Immunocompromised Recipients. Always verify the irradiation status.
